one_line_takeaway: GGuard (a QLoRA-tuned Gemma3-12B on the newly unified 131K-instance, 106-subcategory GuardEval corpus) beats OpenAI Moderator and Llama Guard3 by roughly 0.2 macro-F1 on prompt/response safety classification and generalizes better cross-domain than WildGuard, but exhibits substantial over-blocking (FPR gaps of 0.36-0.49) against Black/African, Muslim, Middle Eastern, and LGBTQ+ identity content.
key_finding: On the primary GuardEval test set, GGuard reaches macro-F1 0.832 for prompt classification versus 0.64 (OpenAI Moderator) and 0.61 (Llama Guard3), and 0.794 for response classification where OpenAI and Llama Guard both score below 0.60 (Table 5/Table 4: overall GuardEval Delta-F1 vs OAI +0.204, vs Llama +0.197, vs WildGuard +0.604, p<0.001 via bootstrap testing except where marked non-significant). Cross-benchmark generalization: GGuard maintains ~0.860 F1 across datasets while WildGuard, despite scoring 0.889 on its own in-domain test set, drops to 0.278 F1 on GuardEval and 0.810 on Nemo-Safety. On five external multi-perspective benchmarks (Table 6: Xtest, PolygloToxicity, OLID, TweetEval, HateCheck) GGuard averages 0.782 F1 versus 0.703 (OpenAI) and 0.66 (Llama Guard3), though Llama Guard3 individually beats GGuard on Xtest (0.904 vs 0.864). Cross-architecture ablation (Table 7) shows fine-tuning other base models (Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen2.5, Phi-4, Gemma) on GuardEval yields large gains on some benchmarks (e.g., Qwen2.5 on ToxicChat prompt F1 rises from 0.18 to 0.818, +354.4%) but occasional regressions for Phi-4 (BeaverTails prompt F1 falls from 0.76 to 0.653, -14.1%). Calibration: post-hoc temperature scaling (T~3.0) reduces ECE from 0.1203 to 0.0554 and Brier score from 0.1635 to 0.1485 without changing accuracy (0.79). Fairness analysis (using nonbinary gender as reference) finds Black/African or Muslim identity content has FPR increases of 0.45-0.49 and Middle Eastern/LGBTQ+ identities 0.36-0.38, with overall mean FPR gaps of 0.384 (identity) and 0.432 (toxicity nuance), i.e., GGuard over-blocks content associated with marginalized identities and nuanced (non-profane) toxicity substantially more than it under-detects (FNR gap <0.12). ShieldGemma was excluded from primary statistical comparison due to inconsistent/near-zero logits when multiple policy rules are queried jointly. A preliminary multimodal extension (GGuard evaluated zero-shot via Gemma3's vision capability on UnsafeBench, an 11-category real+AI-generated image benchmark) shows 'competitive' performance versus Gemma3-12 and GPT-4V baselines despite no vision-specific moderation training, but the paper explicitly flags this as preliminary and non-rigorous.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is threefold: (1) GuardEval, a unified benchmark/training corpus of 131,708 prompts and 47,291 responses (106 fine-grained subcategories collapsing to a 23-category S1-S23 policy taxonomy) built by harmonizing 13 existing moderation datasets (Beavertails, Coconut/CoCoNot, HateBase, MedSafety, MetaHate, Nemo-Safety, Prism, Prosocial, SocialChemistry, ToxicChat, UltraSafety, WildGuard, WildJailBreak) into a common safe/unsafe binary label plus a held-out ~6,000-sample GuardEval test set; (2) GGuard (GemmaGuard), a QLoRA (4-bit, r=16, alpha=16, dropout=0.05) fine-tune of Gemma3-12B on GuardEval trained for 1,000 steps (effective batch 256, AdamW-8bit, lr 2e-5, 48 GPU-hours on an RTX 5000 24GB) that performs joint prompt- and response-level moderation with a fixed taxonomy-anchored chat template; (3) a systematic empirical evaluation of GGuard against SOTA moderators (OpenAI Moderation API, Llama Guard3, WildGuard, ShieldGemma2, Nemo-Guard) across in-domain and out-of-domain benchmarks, including bootstrap significance testing, calibration analysis, fairness-gap analysis, and a preliminary multimodal (image) moderation extension using UnsafeBench.
threat_model: The paper explicitly defines a threat model in Section 4.3: a black-box, prompt-side adversary that seeks to elicit disallowed/policy-violating content by crafting inputs (direct harmful requests, obfuscation, role-play, instruction injection, adversarial jailbreak prompts) that bypass moderation guardrails, without access to model parameters or internal confidence scores, interacting only via API-style queries. Evaluation is dataset-driven (one moderation decision per benchmark instance, B=1) rather than an interactive red-teaming loop; cross-attack sensitivity is approximated by testing the same moderators across multiple datasets differing in attack style/distribution rather than aggregating into one robustness score. This is a genuine, paper-stated adversarial framing, not one imposed by the reviewer.
limitations: Paper-stated limitations (Section 8): evaluation is restricted to English-language text despite the base model's multimodal capacity; a 'taxonomic overlap' problem persists at the fine-grained (106/23-category) level where the model correctly flags content as unsafe but assigns semantically adjacent rather than exact-match subcategories (e.g., confusing Criminal Planning/S3 with Illegal Activity/S15, or Harassment with Hate Speech); evaluation relies on static benchmarks (single-shot, B=1) rather than dynamic/interactive red-teaming, which may not capture evolving jailbreak strategies; comprehensive quantitative fairness audits across demographic subgroups are described as 'very limited' even though the paper itself reports specific FPR-gap numbers, an internal tension the authors acknowledge; and the multimodal evaluation is explicitly preliminary, lacking a large-scale unified multimodal moderation benchmark and training pipeline.

datasets_used: GuardEval (this paper's unified corpus: 131,708 prompts, 47,291 responses, 106 fine-grained / 23 coarse categories, built from 13 source datasets)
External evaluation sets: HateCheck, Xtest, PolygloToxicity, TweetEval, OffensiveLang, OLID
UnsafeBench (11-category multimodal image moderation benchmark, real + AI-generated, for the preliminary multimodal extension)
quantitative_results: GGuard macro-F1 on GuardEval: prompt 0.832 vs OpenAI Moderator 0.64 and Llama Guard 0.61
GGuard macro-F1 response classification: 0.794 vs OpenAI/Llama Guard both <0.60
GuardEval overall Delta-F1 vs OAI +0.204, vs Llama +0.197, vs WildGuard +0.604, vs ShieldGemma +0.115 (p<0.001, Table 4)
WildGuard: 0.889 F1 on its own in-domain test set but only 0.278 F1 on GuardEval and 0.810 on Nemo-Safety; GGuard maintains ~0.860 F1 across datasets
Table 5: GuardEval prompt task macro P/R/F1/AUC = 0.783/0.771/0.776/0.839; response task macro P/R/F1/AUC = 0.796/0.705/0.729/0.745
External benchmark average F1 (Table 6): GGuard 0.782, OpenAI 0.703, LlamaGuard3 0.66; individual best: LlamaGuard3 on Xtest 0.904, OpenAI on PolygloToxicity 0.78 and OLID 0.73

Ablation (Table 7): Qwen2.5 fine-tuned on GuardEval improves ToxicChat prompt F1 from 0.18 (baseline) to 0.818 (+354.4%); Phi-4 regresses on BeaverTails prompt F1 from 0.76 to 0.653 (-14.1%) and on WildGuard prompt F1 from 0.86 to 0.714 (-17.0%)
Calibration (Table 8): ECE 0.1203 -> 0.0554, Brier 0.1635 -> 0.1485 after temperature scaling (T~3.0); accuracy unchanged at 0.79

Operating points (ROC, Fig 3a): balanced threshold tau=0.55 gives F1 0.826 (Recall 0.865, Precision 0.790); conservative tau=0.30 gives Recall 0.900, false-allow rate 0.100, false-block rate 0.313; permissive tau=0.95 gives false-block rate 0.083, false-allow rate 0.352

Fairness gaps: FPR increase of 0.45-0.49 for Black/African or Muslim identities and 0.36-0.38 for Middle Eastern/LGBTQ+ identities (vs nonbinary-gender reference); mean FPR gap 0.384 (identity) and 0.432 (toxicity nuance); FNR mean gap <0.12
Data integrity audit: 135 prompt near-duplicate leaks (2.05%) and 67 response leaks (1.02%) at Jaccard 0.7, 202 total purged; mean inter-source overlap 0.23%
baselines_compared: ShieldGemma2 (Gemma2-7B based, 4 categories) - excluded from primary statistical comparison due to unstable/near-zero logits under joint multi-policy prompting
NVIDIA Nemo-Guard / Llama Nemotron Safety Guard V2 (23 categories)
Cross-architecture fine-tuning ablation baselines: base (non-fine-tuned) Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen2.5, Phi-4, Gemma, each also fine-tuned on GuardEval for comparison
GPT-4V (reported from UnsafeBench source, used only in the preliminary multimodal comparison, not independently re-run)
